title: Clinical Psychologist
how_to_become: Clinical psychology is studied at postgraduate level. You'll need an undergraduate honours degree in psychology and then complete a doctorate in clinical psychology approved by the HCPC.
description: Clinical psychologists use psychological methods and research to offer evidence-based help, supporting people to make positive changes to their lives. They carry out research and evaluation, provide training and supervision, and support other clinicians to deliver psychological therapies.
role_overview: As a clinical psychologist, your aim is to reduce distress and improve the psychological wellbeing of clients with a variety of mental and physical health conditions including depression, anxiety, eating disorders, neurological disorders, learning disabilities, psychosis, and offending behaviour.
conditions_treated: Neurological disorders including dementia or stroke

entry_requirements.overview: At least a 2:1 honours degree from a BPS-accredited psychology programme is required. A conversion course can provide the GBC for those with other degree subjects.
entry_requirements.widening_access: SWAP supports adult learners with few or no qualifications to enter higher education.

work_experience: Work placements and volunteering can provide healthcare experience and help when applying to university, college, or a new job with NHSScotland.

education_and_training.doctorate_duration: 3 years full-time. Programmes must be approved by the HCPC.
education_and_training.registration: On qualifying and registering with the HCPC, you can use the title 'practitioner psychologist' and apply to become a chartered BPS member (CPsychol).

learning_and_development.cpd_requirement: An average of 40 hours of CPD per calendar year over a 3-year period is required to maintain HCPC registration.
cpd_activities: Personal psychological counselling for professional purposes

progression_roles: Expert witness and legal report writing

professional_bodies: British Psychological Society (BPS) — mandatory for Graduate Basis for Chartership
Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC) — mandatory registration

chunks.text: To become a clinical psychologist, complete a BPS-accredited undergraduate psychology degree (at least 2:1), then a 3-year HCPC-approved doctorate in clinical psychology at the University of Edinburgh or University of Glasgow.

Clinical psychologists use psychological methods and evidence-based research to help people make positive changes. They carry out research, provide training and supervision, and support other clinicians to deliver psychological therapies.

Clinical psychologists work with conditions including depression, anxiety, eating disorders, neurological disorders, dementia, stroke, learning disabilities, psychosis, addictive behaviours, and offending behaviour.

At least a 2:1 honours degree from a BPS-accredited psychology programme is required. A conversion course can provide the GBC. Useful school subjects include Psychology, Religious and Moral Studies, English, Human Biology, and Maths.
An average of 40 hours CPD per year over a 3-year period is required to maintain HCPC registration, including training courses, supervision, and reflective practice.
Clinical psychologists can progress to principal clinical psychologist, clinical management, neuropsychology (via BPS QiCN), academic research, teaching, or expert witness roles.
Clinical psychologists must be HCPC-registered and can join the BPS at student, graduate, and chartered membership levels.
